title: Seven lessons from founders building robotics businesses set to scale
gist: Three applied-robotics founders (ex-Waymo/Mind Robotics, Bellboy Robotics, Covariant) argue the winning playbook is full-stack ownership and vertical flexibility while hardware embodiment remains unproven, that a missing 'field deployment engineer' role determines real-world success, and that off-the-shelf vision/language models are actively slowing robotics progress because they weren't built for physical deployment.

insights.claim: The strongest signal for picking a robotics vertical is acute, persistent staffing pain that makes buyers willing to adopt unproven technology, as seen in hotels where one 600-room property runs on 150 staff per day just to turn over rooms.
insights.speaker: Christine Deakers
insights.so_what: Investors evaluating applied-robotics startups should weight labor-scarcity intensity in the target vertical as a leading indicator of adoption speed, not just TAM size.
insights.quote: The best places to build are where there is enough pain for robotics to be welcome.
insights.locator: Build where buyers are primed to embrace robotics

insights.claim: Structured, repeatable environments (predictable room layouts, controlled lighting) make a vertical easier to automate and the resulting task competency transfers to adjacent industries, as shown by Bellboy moving from hotel room turnover to laundry sorting to event-venue table setup.
insights.speaker: Christine Deakers
insights.so_what: Founders and investors should look for verticals where structure lowers deployment risk, then expect (and value) cross-industry expansion via shared task types rather than shared industry labels.
insights.locator: Hotels made an ideal vertical for several other reasons

insights.claim: Covariant chose logistics as its initial vertical because tasks were semi-structured and large-scale, but the decisive and initially underappreciated factor was the intensity of customer urgency to solve the problem.
insights.speaker: Christine Deakers
insights.so_what: When diligencing a robotics startup's vertical choice, probe for evidence of customer urgency (not just task structure) since it predicts sales velocity and deployment persistence.
insights.quote: What we didn't know at the time that turned out to make it a really strong vertical to start with was the degree of customer urgency.
insights.locator: Ted Stinson, former CEO of Covariant, remembers having customer conversations

insights.claim: Covariant initially tried to stay a pure model/intelligence company partnering with automation integrators, but found this strategy limited both velocity and customer insight, forcing a pivot to owning hardware, deployment, and the customer relationship end-to-end.
insights.speaker: Christine Deakers
insights.so_what: Robotics investors should be skeptical of 'pure software/model layer' robotics theses; historically the full-stack integrated model has won on speed and customer feedback loops.
insights.quote: The velocity and the customer insight were materially limiting to our ability to build a company.
insights.locator: Building full-stack offers the deepest customer proximity

insights.claim: The robotics ecosystem has matured enough since Covariant's founding roughly seven years ago that partnering with traditional automation/integration companies is now a genuinely viable strategic option, not just a fallback.
insights.speaker: Christine Deakers
insights.so_what: Watch for a new generation of robotics startups successfully using partnership-led go-to-market where full-stack ownership was previously assumed necessary — this could lower capital intensity for new entrants.
insights.quote: I see more and more examples of more traditional robotics and integration companies who have the potential to be great partners.
insights.locator: However, Ted is reluctant to generalize his learning

insights.claim: Bets on either dominant vertical robotics players or eventual market consolidation both wrongly assume reliable, general-purpose embodiment hardware already exists; since no proven embodiment solves all tasks yet, companies that own more of the stack and iterate across layers together will outpace specialists.
insights.speaker: Christine Deakers
insights.so_what: Investors should discount pure-play thesis bets on either 'vertical robotics winners' or 'consolidation' narratives until embodiment hardware reliability is proven; full-stack generalists remain better positioned near-term.
insights.quote: There is no clear proven embodiment that does everything right yet.
insights.locator: Don't define your vertical or your stack too early

insights.claim: Humanoid robots are commonly claimed as a universal solution to embodiment, but this is difficult to achieve in practice, according to a former senior Waymo engineer now at Mind Robotics.
insights.speaker: Christine Deakers
insights.so_what: Treat humanoid-robot 'universal embodiment' narratives skeptically when evaluating hardware bets; the operator-level view is that this remains unsolved.
insights.quote: You could claim that humanoids will be able to solve all the problems, but this is hard in practice.
insights.locator: Don't define your vertical or your stack too early

insights.claim: The durable competitive moat in applied robotics is not the industry vertical but accumulated, building-specific operational context (e.g., knowing elevator three is slower than elevator two), which is difficult for competitors to replicate and may transfer across use cases better than raw task capability.
insights.speaker: Christine Deakers
insights.so_what: When assessing defensibility of a robotics startup, weight proprietary site-specific operational data/context accumulation over vertical market share or task-level IP.
insights.locator: Sandy's experience at Bellboy illustrates what this looks like on the ground

themes.theme: The missing hire: field deployment engineer

insights.claim: Most applied robotics companies default to hiring researchers and engineers first, but the role that actually determines deployment success or failure — managing the gap between lab performance and real-world operation — has no established job description and is routinely under-hired for.
insights.speaker: Christine Deakers
insights.so_what: When diligencing robotics team composition, check whether the company has explicitly staffed a lab-to-field translation function; its absence is a leading indicator of deployment risk.
insights.locator: The hardest role to fill in applied robotics doesn't have a job description yet

insights.claim: Covariant learned it needed a dedicated 'field deployment engineer' role after elite researchers resisted spending four straight weeks at a distribution center in Louisville, Kentucky, revealing that real-world edge cases (things happening once an hour or twice a week) require a distinct skill set combining technical capability, customer empathy, and communication back to engineering.
insights.speaker: Christine Deakers
insights.so_what: A new, currently undefined labor category ('field deployment engineer') is emerging in applied robotics; investors should watch for startups and staffing/training platforms that formalize and scale this role as a moat and hiring bottleneck.
insights.quote: We challenged some of our most elite researchers. They didn't like doing four weeks straight at a distribution center in Louisville, Kentucky.
insights.locator: We learned that this role needed to exist the hard way

themes.theme: Fundraising narrative shift in physical AI

insights.claim: As recently as roughly early 2025, convincing investors that physical AI was nearing a 'ChatGPT moment' was genuinely difficult; Bellboy Robotics' first funding round came not from a VC but from an individual owner of 170 hotels who lived with the staffing problem daily, providing its first real-world deployment proof point.
insights.speaker: Christine Deakers
insights.so_what: Strategic/industry-insider angel checks from operators with acute pain points can be a faster path to first deployment proof than traditional VC for applied robotics, and a template other founders may replicate.
insights.quote: Today, there is a market with a real need and we have proof we can deploy it, so there is a clear path to scale.
insights.locator: Domain depth and conviction are essential to fundraising efforts

insights.claim: Now that applied robotics viability is broadly accepted, the fundraising differentiator has shifted from proving feasibility to demonstrating both deep domain expertise and genuine long-range founder vision ('being a dreamer').
insights.speaker: Christine Deakers
insights.so_what: Investors screening robotics founders should weight long-horizon vision and domain conviction more heavily now that base-rate feasibility skepticism has faded as a differentiator.
insights.quote: Nowadays there is enough evidence that applied robotics is viable. Now people are looking for somebody who not only understands the use case but is also a dreamer.
insights.locator: Yet past deployment successes and domain depth alone aren't enough

insights.claim: Most robotics companies are building on foundation models designed for language and vision rather than physical deployment, and the common approach of adding data and fine-tuning/pre-training on top of these borrowed models is actively slowing progress industry-wide.
insights.speaker: Christine Deakers
insights.so_what: Robotics-specific foundation models (trained natively on physical/embodied data) represent a differentiated infrastructure opportunity versus retrofitted vision-language models; investors should distinguish startups building native data pipelines from those bolting on fine-tunes.
insights.quote: A lot of companies are building on top of existing things that have not necessarily been built for robotics... it's slowing everybody down.
insights.locator: Every robotics company is paying the cost of building on models

insights.claim: Mind Robotics' core strategic bet is collecting egocentric training data directly on Rivian's plant floor rather than relying on borrowed or generic training data, treating proprietary first-party physical data collection as the foundation for reaching high performance.
insights.speaker: Christine Deakers
insights.so_what: Mind Robotics' partnership/access to Rivian plant floors is a proprietary data moat worth tracking; similar direct-site data-collection deals could be a differentiator to screen for in other robotics startups.
insights.quote: Our entire strategy is based on the ability to collect data on the Rivian plants.
insights.locator: At Mind Robotics, the response is deliberate

insights.claim: Beyond imitation learning, pushing hard robotics tasks into reinforcement learning requires building complex infrastructure from scratch because no established playbook or 'recipe' exists yet, representing a second major industry-wide bottleneck.
insights.speaker: Christine Deakers
insights.so_what: A standardized RL infrastructure/playbook for robotics is a white-space opportunity; whoever productizes it first could become critical picks-and-shovels infrastructure for the sector.
insights.quote: I'm really looking for that recipe. Having something like that would benefit everybody.
insights.locator: The second bottleneck compounds the first

insights.claim: Factories and warehouses have historically been built for humans with robots retrofitted in, but this is expected to reverse toward facilities designed with an automation-first mindset as hardware building blocks become increasingly modular and Lego-like.
insights.speaker: Christine Deakers
insights.so_what: Investors should watch industrial real estate, warehouse, and factory design/construction for early signals of automation-first greenfield builds, which would represent a structural tailwind for robotics hardware and integration vendors.
insights.quote: The way factories and warehouses operate will reflect an automation-first mindset more and more.
insights.locator: Today's factories and warehouses were built for humans

insights.claim: The robotics companies that win the next decade may be defined less by proprietary hardware or models and more by their ability to rapidly integrate and deploy mass-customized systems across a fragmented landscape of specialized hardware and physical-intelligence providers.
insights.speaker: Christine Deakers
insights.so_what: Integration/orchestration layers for heterogeneous robotics hardware could become the highest-value part of the stack, analogous to systems integrators in enterprise IT; watch for startups positioning explicitly as integration platforms.
insights.quote: What I see missing is the ability to rapidly integrate and deploy mass-customized systems.
insights.locator: The robotics companies that win the next decade
